author: @scsft
text:
Yes Robert, this is not something you can get over. It is indeed something you'll have to learn how to live with.
You should know that the real process of mourning usually only starts about half a year after the moment you've lost a person close to you.
That's the point where people usually break down into tears.

You can mitigate that by doing the stuff you love, which in your case, is finding means of harvesting energy out of absolutely everything.

Furthermore, it would be wise to make sure that you find "a tribe", if you don't already have one that is, which can catch you when those really bad days are going to hit you in the near future. This could be as simple as having a "pub night" once every week or something else that you like. Just make sure that it's not a solitary activity.

Good luck cause I'm wishing you all the best.

author: @modifyman6977
text:
Out of all the DIY'rs and explorers and don't think I have ever heard you use the term "Hack."
...and that, to me, is what puts you above most.

I get it. Having your commentator/s (on each shoulder?) in your head never shutting up and reminding you...suggesting that you...begging you...telling you...and irritating you when you want solace and peace. 

It does "slowly" get better...helps if you keep busy...🚴‍♀🚴‍♂🚴
Think about a more comfortable riding bike...Heck make it an e-bike...or...a swytchbike.

But really, those 10 speeds design are not the most comfortable design for leisure riding/touring/exploring.
Look at some other designs...FATTER seat (Your butt will thank you.)...maybe suspension but not a mono-shock...they tend to drain your peddling force. 
A comfortable ride makes the trip more therapeutic and enjoyable.
Unless it's a race. Then a motor for those uphill assists are nice if needed.

An adventure is awaiting you in your next chapter...
...but really...if you were that big of a smoker then get a bike with a motor.
You don't want your brothers having to...

You are very much like them in the passion and excitement that you have while doing your own style of tinkering (we can't quite pin you down as just one thing, can we?). You still have maintained the wonderment of a child when a creation goes your way! That laugh is contagious. You have given so much to all of us, a bit of support and kind words is nothing in comparison. 
The months ahead will be rough, but that boyish charm will carry you through!

author: @soulrobotics
text:
Hi Robert,

I hope you're doing well. I wanted to share some thoughts with you. The chemistry you teach, with copper nanoparticles and all, has always fascinated me. It reminds me that we, in essence, are also like those atoms, creating meaningful bonds and connections. As Saint Exupéry said, these bonds transform us and help us grow.

When we lose one of those connections, the sadness can be overwhelming. But it also opens the door to new adventures and possibilities of connecting in new ways. The heart's memory holds all those beautiful experiences we've lived through, and those memories become part of us, ready to share with the new bonds we form.

I see you're making changes in your life. I'd like to suggest something: baking homemade bread. In one of my videos, I explain how to do it, and although it's not complex and is based on the work of others, it’s my version. Baking sourdough bread can be a very rewarding experience and, in a way, it’s connected to your love of science. I know you'll do it perfectly and creatively. Plus, you can't eat it all by yourself, so you'll have to share it, and that's the key.

Robert, besides being an excellent chemist, you are a very noble person. I'm sure everything will go very well for you.

Greetings from Spain, Dennis @ SOULROBOTICS

author: @Captn_Altblech
text:
I don't know what to say. I myself had some rough months in which I had no time to be creative and I knew I couldn't fit any new ideas into my brain, so I paused watching your videos during that time. Only to come back and see that you had been hit with such a huge loss. You gave me so many ideas and positive thoughts, but in your time of need I wasn't even able to express my condolences.
I'm so sorry, mate!

But it's good to see that you found something that keeps you going!
Be assured you're not alone with that kind of feelings such a loss brings with it!
In the end that's what we do: We keep on going. 

Oh, and btw: Even NOW you gave me some new ideas: Harvesting energy from anywhere possible + bike = some kind of 4 wheeled sailing bike...
Inspiring others, planting ideas. I guess that's just what you do...
Thanks mate! And may loving memories bring you strength and peace.

I've followed you for quite some time and I was amazed at how incredibly productive you were in churning out such informative videos on various topics. Now I know the secret.
I'm curious to know how you complemented each other as a team, especially as a marital team. Would you be willing to be a showcase of how such a team can tackle life's challenges? In today's world where feminism rules and self-entitlement is the norm, you stood out as a marital team where the strengths and weaknesses were organized into a powerhouse of productivity. The moment one partner fell away the whole operation came to a halt, because of the intricate interdependence being totally messed up.
Sorry for my clinical assessment of a highly emotional situation. Hope you will consider the lesson in it being conveyed to a super selfish society.

author: @dennissorensen8765
text:
This was such a nice message, dear Robert. I hope that your biking adventure with your family will reacquaint you with the joy of - and rekindle your passion for - life ... and the many mysteries of the world. 😊🤗

I have thought of you sporadically over the months that have passed, and wondered how you and Luke were doing.
Wondrous really, how that human element works. You have shown such a personal engagement with us and the process of sharing your curiosity and knowledge with us. Teaching and engaging with us faceless strangers out here. It is like having a private professor, passionate about science and discoveries, but also being there. Answering questions, showing gratitude and humility. Being genuine.

You seem like a kind soul, Robert. 
😊

I logged on YouTube today, specifically to search for you, because I have been wondering how you were doing daily these past few days.

I'm glad to see that others have also expressed that they missed you and thought of your wellbeing. 😊

The best wishes to you from Denmark, and thank you for thinking of telling us all how you are doing. Such a nice gesture.

It takes a while to come back. It changes you to lose someone dear. But re-emerge we must, albeit slightly changed, after a period of incubation.

You will find your passion for science and discoveries and your happiness again. You will also very sometimes, even years from now, when remembering the good times and wishing she was there to share some nice moment with. And that is okay. That's how it should be. The legacy our loved ones leave behind in us. 😊🤗 Five years down the road, I have learned to smile while the tears sometimes run down my cheeks. Because it is from dear memories and fondness of what was there for so long not just sorrow of loss. 😊 I think that's how they would want it to be as well. 🤗 Take care while you heal, and we'll all look forward to dive into some interesting subject with you again in the future. 😊

text: I was beginning to worry about you, Good Sir! After seeing the effect of the loss of my Mom on my Dad over the past year and a half or so, I’ve seen how hard it’s been on him. I haven’t had the fortune of having a single woman in that role over my years, so I’ve seen the ebb and flow of my inspirations and lulls of creativity. I hope you’re able to find your drive again, not saying you’ve lost it either, but all the same, you’ve taken a big blow. Whats up to you is whether or not you get back up for the next one, and how you go about doing it! I hope and pray you’ll do as well with your healing as you do with your projects, because you are a great teacher and inspiration to as you’ve seen quite a good number of people.

author: @burntofferings3770
text: Sorry for your loss. It is incredibly hard to carry on when the light of your life goes out. But carry on you must.  You are needed more than ever. Our people are literally perishing from lack of knowledge.  One of the wickedest tricks the rulers have pulled on the Common man is too limit his imagination, thinking, inquiry and experimentation to very confined parameters.  Each one of your videos might spark a further invention or revolutionary refinement.  Every single good mind you touch might well be inspired to become a freethinking curious inventor. It is a huge hoax to believe you can't make a positive difference in our society.  As a multiple patent holder and determined original thinker I have pioneered innovations in several fields and am currently working and researching several more.  Never stop being curious, learning new concepts and trying to invent things to make our society more civilized, safe, peaceful, prosperous and INTERESTING.  Live, learn, laugh and love, be kind, and do no harm. Thanks for all you do Robert .

author: @weedfreer
text:
Good for you Rob.

I recently went up to 16st and change so had to change something.

As such, I took up running.

I grew up on the east coast though… where it’s, well, flat.

Where I live now, it most certainly isn’t.

I used to love athletics, but, hill running is making it hard to enjoy what I’m doing now.

Still, the ability to get your brain into an ‘inner space’ has been just as much a challenge.

You’re right though, it does absolute wonders for your mentality.

It’s a sorry old situation what you’ve been through, but, you’re channeling the bad into something I think everyone can agree is for the good.

Keep at it, I’m certain you’ll reach your goal.  Heck, maybe it’s who’ll you’ll meet along the way that’ll prove it’s worth to you!

So sorry for your loss, Rob. Death is always hardest for those left behind. You'll meet physically again someday - until then she will always be with you - and she will be reminding you everyday in one way or another! Subtle things, hardly noticeable to just anyone but when YOU see them you will know 😊 
Her inspiration and energy is still within you though - and perhaps getting back into your projects again will trigger and encourage this? I suspect either way she'd want you to be doing whatever makes you happy - she'll definitely be looking out for you! 
Good to see your face again bud, best of luck with the cycling! And don't feel pressured to make content - share when it feels right. 
You deserve as much rest and time as you want 💪

You have been well advised and your decision to employ bike riding is an absolutely perfect choice as is gives you escape, routine, exercise, and fresh air:) the old addage of "be not idle, be not alone" should be carved in granite somewhere. The grief of losing your best friend will never leave you. but eventually the tears will turn into smiles as you reminisce about your time together. author: @Andykerrfield
text:
So sorry for your loss, couldn't imagine losing my better half.
This idea might help get you back on the saddle and continue your engineering journey, it's a potential build idea I've been considering for a long bike journey for us out of shapers.
A dual changeover/combination battery ebike, charge one set while using the other and switch over once full or for off-road use and steep hills combine for extra boost.
2 x 36v battery packs should be enough I reckon,
Four position switch, eventually autochanger with boost combo button,
Coils down all forks with magnetic reflectors on the spokes and maybe a small wind turbine mounted under the seat at the back to charge..
The journey I have in mind is from Wye valley (Buckinghamshire) to Wye valley (South Wales), it's about 100 miles following either the Thames and then the Kennett and Avon canal or pick up King Alfred's Way at Reading and then the Ridgeway which passes loads of ancient sites. Either route is about 100 miles and was hoping to cover 30-40 miles per day and take in all the sights.
Do you know of a UK battery company that might already have such a system to save me reinventing the wheel? Any help with this endeavour would be greatly appreciated. Cheers

author: @chrisbingham3289
text:
Parting

It tears my heart and soul apart.

The pain of knowing we must part.

God will wave his mighty arm.

And our souls his will command. 

To leave this earth that we have known.

To stand by him upon his throne.  

 

We will look down from up above.

And see the ones that we once loved. 

The pain we felt now has gone. 

But the ones we left must carry on.

Lift you head and wipe your tears. 

For we are with you have no fears

 

The pain your feeling in your heart.

Is not a pain because you part .

It's just the yearning of your love.

For the one that's past above.

But have no doubt this will subside. 

When comes the time to join their side.

 

C .J .Bingham 2024

author: @LiveWire937
text:
So glad to hear from you again, Robert, and to see how you're handling things. I know too well how hard it can be losing someone who means the absolute world to you. I'm sure I don't have anything to say on the subject that you haven't heard already in these past few months, but suffice to say I think you've got the right/healthiest perspective. Loath as I am to admit it, routine really is key. It was really hard for me to accept, as, historically, I've always really railed against even just the idea of having a routine or schedule... something about doing the same thing 2 days in a row, let alone 30 days, just utterly saps my spirit and depresses me deeply... but if I hadn't figured out how to balance the chaotic disarray that loss had made of my mind against some kind of stability, I doubt if I ever would have been able to move forward. For me it took years instead of months, owing to my stubbornness, to get where you seem to be now emotionally, so it makes me happy to see that you've got a better head on your shoulders about all this than I ever did when the pain was still so fresh.

Still keeping you in my thoughts and prayers, of course. As much as I've enjoyed watching your videos and following your projects over the years, I'd much rather you continue to focus on what you need to do in order to enjoy life rather than trying to jump straight back into the same style/pace of content that you've done before. Don't let anyone make you feel pressured into any one niche, least of all yourself; you're a clever and talented tinkerer to be sure, but I believe most of your audience is here just as much for your knack at presentation and the tenacity you seem to have in everything you do.

text: I have been waiting to give you my condolences for your loss. I went through it 8 years ago. I spent the next 3 years in solitude in my motorhome traveling America. It was then that I discovered your show completely by accident, or so I thought. I'm sure you have no idea how you affect some of us. You gave me something to focus on. Following your lead I was able to understand so many new, old, things. Your also gave me an interest in my roots. My family came to America from England. My grandmothers family, Skelton, came from areas of Northern Yorkshire, New Castle I believe. My Grandfathers family, Routh, came from Yorkshire also. The west riding near Beverly, the village of Routh still stands. In the 1990s my father visited the village and had a meal at the pub and brought back a menu for me. I think I currently possess more varieties of wind and water generators than anyone within 100 miles. I know somewhat of where you have been. I also know what can wait for you a head. My Tanya was a soul mate for 36 years. Her passing was no surprise and took 16 years of treatments. It made us stronger. It was still a surprise when it actually happened... author: @thesentientneuron6550
text: You shone a light in my life. Of course we would offer you our support. Even now, your light shines on someone. I’m sincerely sorry that she passed so suddenly and offer my sincerest condolences. Your approach to life itself has a magical, non-intrusive way of healing and helping others. But even so, I still believe that the first person we should focus on healing and taking care of is ourselves, and that applies equally to you as well, Rob. You’ve taken up a wonderful challenge, so do keep us updated on it if you’re able and willing. You being an old man doesn’t change anything about how inspiring you are. If anything, it’s even more incredible precisely because of what you’re doing at your age. I’m a young guy, and you inspire me to keep on keeping on. Rock on, Rob!

author: @antonia4722

author: @samTollefson
text:
I am so sorry for your loss, Robert.
One thing that has helped me is thinking about how your lost loved one would want you to feel right now. Do they want you to be despondent, sad, and at loose ends with yourself? Would they want you to be rejoiceful, remembering all the beautiful times you had together? Would they want you to move on to new life experiences and grow in new and different directions with some new people and new endeavors? 

You are already hearing some of the answers to those questions!
You will also know that that person's Spirit will always be with you!

Well... Thank you, for your thoughtful discussion... It's a terrible fact that one of your "driving forces" has been taken away, from you, and, dare I say, many of your friends, relatives, and, to a much smaller, but real, extent, from your YouTube "fans"... You sound as though you are working your way to another level, and I know what a heavy load it is... Whatever you choose to do with your life, as it is, and becomes, it's *your life* , but you will always carry with you the love, and influences, of your lady! 
It's also true that time won't quite "heal" the loss, but your life continues itself, and we hope that you will, at last, have moments when you can smile about some little thing that she would have liked, or enjoyed, and that will bring a bit of her back to you.
All the best on the rocky road  to your new self ... (looks like "literally", too!)

author: @thomashverring
text:
I've been thinking about you a lot lately, and occasionally the past three months of course, because such a loss is not something you get over, but you have to get on. And live with it. As you said.

I think getting back into it is definitely the right thing to do. Finding things that get you excited and motivate you—even just to a small degree—is the best way to find your own place in the world as you are now. But she will always be there, to a very high degree just outside of touch for the next few years, which is the painful part, and then slowly as the pleasant memory and someone you can talk to when you feel melancholic (the last bit is not a bad thing!). But the unfairness of it all will come back and bite forever, not as often but still.

We don't want anything else than you getting back on your feet! Videos for your will be received with gratitude, but make them for your own sake before you make them for our sake.

Much love from Denmark!

author: @georgejohnson1498
text:
So happy to see such a nice vid from you. Take care and look after yourself. Just practice for the Norwich Fifty. Lands End to John "o'Groats is too much much for smokers [or even ex-smoker in your case] of our age!

I'd rather watch you survive fifty miles in Norfolk than see a tiny headline on the BBC web reporting your demise. I gave up cycling after more than fifty year two years ago, but have been experimenting with an electric Trike. One hand on the bar and Patterdale Terrier happy under my righty arm. No laws were particularly broken!

So my plan is to scrap my ICE car [Citroen Berlingo van] and get an electric trike, with a front shopping basket for the little dog. She loved it, so it will be no problem to get her into a front shopping [wicker] basket. She is very bright about new things. Everyday for her is a new adventure ...

Your examinations of the relatively simple [in terms of complex modern tech] films about the possible ways of harvesting sustainable energy remain forever a master class in particle solutions.

Anything you might wish too add in future will be joyfully watch here in rural Herefordshire by another old-timer, who has lost a lot, and yet somehow survived.

Best wishes from George
